forecasts.reasoning:
The question asks if there will be more than 10x the baseline number of riots in Maldives in the 30 days before March 8, 2026. Because the Maldives has a very low historical baseline for ACLED-coded 'Riots', the baseline effectively zeroes in on a tiny denominator. The prompt notes that the reference value on Feb 19, 2026, was 1.167. A reference value of ~1.167 mathematically translates to 1 + (x/12) = 1.167 => x/12 = 0.167 => x ≈ 2. This implies there were roughly 2 'Riots' over the prior 360 days.

To fulfill the resolution conditions, there must be at least 10 * 1.167 ≈ 11.67 => 12 'Riots' in the 30-day window from Feb 6, 2026, to Mar 8, 2026. 

At the time of this forecast (March 1, 2026), 23 days of this 30-day window have already passed. Multiple targeted searches for February 2026 unrest, protests, riots, or any violence in the Maldives yield absolutely no current events. There was a wave of anti-government protests in late 2025, but those concluded by November 2025 without generating enough violent events to meaningfully inflate the typical riot baseline. In the absence of any ongoing crisis or even isolated violent events in the news currently, it is exceptionally unlikely that ACLED will code 12 or more riots during this 30-day window. The probability is set at 2% (the hard minimum threshold) due to the complete lack of requisite events.

The question asks if there will be >10 times the baseline rate of 'Explosions/Remote violence' in Guadeloupe. The ACLED reference value is 1.000, meaning there were 0 such events in the last 360 days. To resolve YES, there would need to be 11 or more artillery/IED/airstrike/remote explosives events in Guadeloupe within the 30 days leading up to March 8, 2026. Guadeloupe is not an active war zone, and web searches for 2025/2026 turn up absolutely no mention of armed conflict or remote violence. References to an "explosive situation" in the news pertain entirely to civil unrest and protests. Therefore, it is exceedingly unlikely that 11+ ACLED-defined 'Explosions/Remote violence' events will occur during this period.

The criteria require at least 12 'Violence against civilians' events in Rwanda between Feb 6 and Mar 8, 2026. Rwanda generally experiences extremely low levels of domestic violence, averaging around 2 events per year. Exhaustive searches for protests, riots, or civilian attacks within Rwanda during early 2026 yielded no result. All recent violence involving Rwanda pertains to its backing of M23 rebels in the Democratic Republic of Congo or forces in Mozambique, which ACLED codes under those respective countries. Consequently, the likelihood of a massive undocumented spike in domestic violence against civilians reaching the threshold is essentially zero.

The question essentially resolves YES if there is at least one political violence fatality in Jordan between Feb 6, 2026, and March 8, 2026. The historical 30-day average is 0.833, implying ~10 fatalities per year. However, analysis of past incidents (drone strikes, border clashes) shows violence in Jordan is highly clustered (typically 3-5 deaths per incident). This means there are only about 2-3 fatal incidents per year. As of March 1, 23 days of the 30-day window have passed, and extensive searches in both English and Arabic across major wire services and Jordanian media (Petra, Jordan Times) show no reports of political violence or border clash fatalities in this period. With only 7 days remaining, the baseline probability of a new fatal incident is approximately 6-7%. Factoring in a ~4-5% chance of an unobserved low-profile death that ACLED might catalog retrospectively, the overall probability is estimated at 12%.

The question asks if there will be more than 10 times as many ACLED 'Riots' in Vietnam in the 30 days prior to March 8, 2026 as compared to the baseline (which is currently 1.000, implying exactly 0 riots over the past 360 days). To resolve YES, there would need to be at least 11 recorded riots in Vietnam during February/early March 2026. Given Vietnam's strict authoritarian control and lack of any recent news regarding mass protests or civil unrest, an explosion of 11 violence-involved riots in a single month would represent a massive, unprecedented crisis. Multiple web searches for protests, riots, or any unrest in Vietnam in 2025 and 2026 returned no evidence of such events occurring. Therefore, the probability of reaching this high threshold is exceptionally low.

Tuvalu is an extremely small and peaceful nation with a population of ~11,000 and no organized armed groups. The most recent ACLED data value cited (as of 2026-02-19) is 1.000, confirming that there were zero recorded incidents of violence against civilians over the past 360 days. The question requires more than 10 incidents of 'Violence against civilians' in Tuvalu in a 30-day window (since 10 * 1 = 10, so we need >10). Given the absolute absence of armed groups, political violence, or civil unrest—supported by recent news searches covering up through March 2026 that solely report on climate migration, an upcoming cultural festival, and peaceful economic conditions—the probability of 11 or more armed attacks on civilians in Tuvalu within a single month is negligible. I estimate this at the minimum forecastable probability of 0.02.

The base rate of 3.167 protests per 30 days implies about 38 protests over the 360-day window from March 2025 to Feb 2026. If these were uniformly distributed, a Poisson distribution gives a ~39% chance of seeing 4 or more protests in the target 30-day period (which is required to resolve YES). However, it is highly likely that these protests were clustered, perhaps around the water-related unrest seen in late March 2025. Given that targeted web searches found absolutely no reports of protests, strikes, or civil unrest in Martinique during January or February 2026, the current underlying rate of protests is very likely lower than the annual average. Without an active wave of unrest, the probability of hitting 4 or more protests in the single month leading up to March 8, 2026, is well below the Poisson generic expectation. I estimate this at 18%.

The 360-day average of 14.500 ACLED fatalities per 30-day period largely reflects a period when Trinidad and Tobago was under an extended State of Emergency (SoE) that successfully suppressed gang violence by up to 60%. This SoE expired on January 31, 2026, and a replacement ZOSO bill to maintain enhanced police powers was rejected by the Senate on January 28, 2026. Without these extra powers, violence is already showing signs of rebounding in February 2026, with news reports confirming double murders and gang-related attacks (e.g., Feb 22, 2026). Furthermore, the Prime Minister promised a 'brutal' post-SoE response, which increases the likelihood of fatal police clashes—events specifically tracked by ACLED. Because the baseline of 14.5 is artificially low due to the strict SoE, and the forecast window (Feb 6 – Mar 8, 2026) perfectly aligns with the immediate post-SoE rebound period, the probability of exceeding the 14.5 average is high.

The base rate of protests in Guam is low, with the 360-day baseline average as of Feb 19 standing at 0.833 (exactly 10 events). A confirmed protest took place on Thursday, Feb 26, 2026, outside Adelup (the governor's complex), where Prutehi Guåhan activists held signs and chanted against BOEM's deep-sea mining meetings. This single event strictly occurred within the required 30-day period (Feb 6, 2026 to March 7, 2026). Including this event raises the 360-day total to 11 (or an average of 0.916). Since the 30-day count must be greater than this baseline average, 1 event is sufficient to resolve the question as YES (1 > 0.916). Any additional unrecorded protests in the final window would only increase the margin. The only risk for a NO resolution is if ACLED fails to code the documented Feb 26 protest or randomly adds previous undocumented events spanning back several months that push the background average to >=1.0 (12+ events). Given ACLED's consistent capture of previous Prutehi Guåhan actions in Guam, I assign a high probability of YES.

The base rate of violence in Anguilla is exceptionally low—as noted in the background, the reference value as of Feb 2026 is exactly 1.000, meaning there were 0 ACLED fatalities in the preceding 360 days. To trigger a "YES" resolution, there would need to be at least 11 ACLED-classified fatalities (more than 10 * 1.0) in the 30 days leading up to Mar 8, 2026. Anguilla has a population of ~15,000, and a search for violence or deaths in 2025 and 2026 yielded only a couple of individual homicides (which themselves deeply shook the community). No evidence of any mass casualty event, gang war, or political violence with 10+ fatalities was found in late 2025 or current 2026 news. Given the tiny size of the territory and lack of any news reflecting such a massive unprecedented escalation, the probability is near 0.

Comprehensive searches for drone crashes, missiles, or explosions in Moldova and Transnistria in February 2026 only found one isolated incident (a Russian drone found on February 6, 2026). Without a massive, unprecedented spike in conflict—which the news definitively rules out—there is no plausible path to reaching 11+ explosions/remote violence events in this 30-day period. Therefore, the probability is extremely low.

The threshold requires at least 13 protests in a 30-day window in the Caribbean Netherlands (Bonaire, Saba, Sint Eustatius). The population is approximately 30,000 across all three islands. The historical base rate for protests is incredibly low, averaging about 3 per year according to the 1.250 reference value. Without a massive crisis driving near-daily protests, hitting this 10x threshold is virtually impossible.

Niue is a small island nation with a population of approximately 1,900 people. The recorded reference value on February 19, 2026, was exactly 1.0, which means there were 0 fatalities over the past year. In order for the question to resolve positively, there would need to be 11 or more fatalities in ACLED-tracked events (political violence, riots, protests, battles) within a 30-day window. Eleven fatalities from political violence in a nation of under 2,000 would be an unprecedented catastrophe and major international news. Extensive searches show absolutely no indication of any violence, let alone a mass fatality event, occurring in Niue in early 2026. Given the complete lack of any relevant political conflict and the extremely stable nature of the country, the chances of such an event occurring in the final week before resolution—or having occurred unnoticed in the past three weeks—are infinitesimally small. I assign the minimum allowable probability.

The 30-day average of 'Explosions/Remote violence' in Rwanda over the 360 days preceding early 2026 is officially recorded as 0.000 by ACLED. This establishes a baseline that such events are extremely rare in Rwanda, with precisely zero occurring in the preceding year. For the question to resolve positively, at least one explosive or remote violence event must occur within Rwanda's borders between February 6, 2026, and March 7, 2026. 

Based on comprehensive searches of recent news and conflict trackers up to the forecast date (March 1, 2026), there have been no credible reports of explosions, cross-border shelling, or drone strikes within Rwandan territory over the 23 elapsed days of the 30-day window. While there is active and escalating conflict just across the border in the DRC involving the M23 rebels and the Congolese army—including a drone strike in DRC on February 24—no spillover violence hitting Rwanda has been recorded during this period.

Given the base rate of nearly zero, the fact that 75% of the target window has already elapsed without incident, and the high likelihood that any cross-border shelling into Rwanda would generate significant news coverage, the probability that an event will happen in the remaining 7 days (or occurred but has not yet been reported) is very low. I am estimating the final probability at 5%, the minimum allowed threshold.

The resolution depends on whether the count of 'Strategic developments' in Poland from Feb 6 to Mar 8, 2026 exceeds the preceding 360-day average (currently 7.917/month). 'Strategic developments' do NOT include standard protests or riots; they reflect specific non-violent milestones like key arrests, military base changes, agreements, or major troop deployments. 

The preceding 360 days (March 2025 – March 2026) likely saw inflated counts of strategic developments due to two major periods of activity:
1. Poland's Presidential elections (May 2025), which often correspond with coalition 'Agreements' and administrative changes.
2. The Russia-Belarus "Zapad" military exercises in September 2025, during which Poland closed its border with Belarus and made espionage arrests, leading to troop deployments and other relevant coding events.

Because these spikes inflate the 360-day average (bringing the mean up to ~95 events for the year), an ordinary month would have to be exceptionally active to beat this average. Furthermore, variables like migrant border crossings (which trigger smuggling arrests and border guard deployments) are highly seasonal and drop significantly in the harsh winter months of February and March. 

While recent news indicates a few isolated 'Strategic developments' in late February 2026—such as the arrest of a Belarusian spy and of a group smuggling sanctioned equipment to Russia—these singular events are unlikely to tally up to the required 8 or 9 events needed to surpass the highly inflated annual baseline. Because the distribution of such events is right-skewed (median < mean) and we are comparing a slow winter month against an active baseline year, the probability of a YES resolution is roughly 25-30%.

The base rate of Strategic Developments in Montenegro is extremely low (average of ~1.25 per month based on the reference value 2.25 being one plus the average). To surpass 10x the reference value, there would need to be 23 or more 'Strategic development' events in the 30 days leading up to March 8, 2026. ACLED defines Strategic Developments as distinct non-violent actions like troop movements, high-profile arrests, weapons seizures, and looting. While the arrest of former minister Vesna Bratić occurred in mid-February 2026, there is no evidence of a widespread wave of similar events (22+ additional events) occurring in Montenegro during the timeframe. A 10x multiplier over a 30-day window is mathematically extremely difficult to reach without a severe domestic crisis or outright conflict (events such as protests and riots are categorized differently and would not count anyway). I assign a 2% probability.

The 30-day window for counting battles runs from approximately Feb 6, 2026 to Mar 7, 2026. As of the forecast date (Mar 1, 2026), 23 days have passed with no news reports of any armed conflict, coups, or rebel activity in Equatorial Guinea. The average number of battles in the preceding 360 days was 0, meaning the threshold to resolve positive is 11 or more battles in this 30-day window. Equatorial Guinea is a small, tightly-controlled country with typically zero battles per year in the ACLED dataset. The base rate for 'Strategic developments' in Hungary is ~2.42 per 30 days. For the 30-day window (Feb 6 - Mar 7, 2026) to exceed this average, there need to be at least 3 events. Under a Poisson distribution with lambda = 2.417, the probability of 3 or more events is ~44%. However, this 30-day window falls squarely in the crucial final pre-election campaign period for Hungary's April 12, 2026 parliamentary election. In highly contested elections, ACLED often records an increase in Strategic developments due to incidents like the defacement of political posters/offices (coded as 'Looting/property destruction'), minor political arrests, and formations of new political/activist movements ('Change to group/activity'). With Viktor Orbán facing his tightest challenge from Péter Magyar's Tisza party, political polarization and campaign activity are exceptionally high. Assuming the rate of events temporarily increases during this heated campaign month to a lambda of ~3.0, the probability of observing 3 or more events rises to roughly 58%.

However, the event took place "near Bequia", which is in St. Vincent and the Grenadines. According to ACLED's location-based methodology, this event will be geolocated and assigned to St. Vincent and the Grenadines, not Saint Lucia. While tensions in St. Lucia are running high over the incident, the likelihood of a localized explosive event occurring before March 8 or the strike being mistakenly coded to Saint Lucia remains low.

To resolve YES, Mauritania would need over 10 times its baseline average of 'Strategic developments', meaning ~24 or more events recorded by ACLED in just 30 days (Feb 6 – Mar 8, 2026). 'Strategic developments' usually encompass arrests, troop movements, looted infrastructure, etc. Such a massive volume of events would only occur during a massive political crisis, coup d'état, or the sudden outbreak of nationwide conflict. A comprehensive search of Jan/Feb 2026 news for Mauritania reveals no such events — no elections, no major crackdowns, and no new conflict fronts. In fact, standard news involves economic development and stability. Mauritania remains remarkably resilient to the violence ravaging the Sahel (Mali, Burkina Faso), and without any signs of a crisis, it is near-impossible that the number of strategic developments will spike to 10+ times normal. I assign the lowest allowable probability (0.02).

The Caribbean Netherlands (Bonaire, Sint Eustatius, and Saba) has a very small population (<30,000) and is highly stable. An extensive search for the key time window (Feb 6 - Mar 7, 2026) revealed general police understaffing and a single normal homicide, but zero evidence of any events matching ACLED's criteria. Given the structural absence of armed groups and historical rate of zero, the probability is extremely low and set at the minimum allowed bound of 2%.

For 11+ instances of violence against civilians by an organized armed group to randomly erupt within a 30-day period in Gibraltar—especially with no news of any such events occurring in February 2026 to date—is virtually impossible. The 360-day window from March 2025 to February 2026 has an average of ~146.6 battles per 30-day period. While the baseline probability of any 30-day period exceeding the 360-day average is typically around 40-45% due to the bursty right-skewed nature of conflict data (where massive spikes pull the mean up above the median), current trends in the DRC point to an actively escalating conflict *right now*. An ongoing major offensive by M23 and other groups that captured Uvira in December 2025 has transitioned into further 'escalated conflict and intensified M23 attacks' through January and February 2026, according to recent UN OCHA and ACLED reports. Because the highest intensity of the conflict appears to be occurring at the very end of the 360-day window (and extending into the first week of March), the focal 30-day period (Feb 6 - Mar 7, 2026) is highly likely to feature an above-average number of battle events compared to the year-long baseline, which includes presumably quieter periods in mid-2025.

To resolve YES, the 30-day period from Feb 6 to Mar 7, 2026, must have strictly more 'Strategic developments' than the 30-day average over the preceding 360 days (3.917), meaning at least 4 events are required. The 360-day reference period (Mar 2025 to Feb 2026) includes the October 2025 presidential election and May 2025 coup rumors, which likely heavily skewed the average upwards with pre-election crackdowns, arrests, and security deployments. Conflict event counts are typically overdispersed, meaning the median is lower than the mean; a quiet post-election month shouldn't be expected to exceed an election-inflated average. Through extensive searching of both English and French news sources for Feb 2026, I found no notable reports of arrests, troop movements, weapons seizures, or border incidents in Ivory Coast that would typically fall under ACLED's 'Strategic developments'. While ACLED often captures low-level events missed by international and major local media (e.g., related to northern border counter-terrorism efforts), the complete lack of visible incidents over the first 24 days of the 30-day window makes it highly unlikely that the 4-event threshold will be met.

The ICE BofA CCC & Lower US High Yield Index saw a rapid surge in yields from 12.42% on Feb 20, 2026 to 13.0% by Feb 28, mirroring a significant late-February stock market selloff. Corporate bond yields generally follow a random walk over short horizons, meaning the probability of an upward move is roughly equal to a downward move (~50%). However, there is a non-trivial probability that the yield remains exactly the same (a tie, as happened between Feb 20 and Feb 27), in which case the question resolves to NO (it asks if the yield will have *increased*). Over a 1-week horizon (March 1 to March 8), the chance of a tie is higher than over a 1-month horizon, making the likelihood of a strict increase slightly below 50% (around 47%). Recent momentum over the past month is slightly down, but flat over the past week. Over the 1-month horizon (March 31), the chance of a tie shrinks and broader consensus for the year anticipates slight steepening of the yield curve, pushing the probability of an increase closer to a pure coin flip (49%). Therefore, the probabilities are set below 50%, moving closer to 0.5 for the more distant date.

The baseline price (the last available data before/on 2026-03-01) is $71.32 from Friday, Feb 27. Over the weekend of March 1, major news broke of a US-Israeli conflict with Iran disrupting Middle East shipments, driving over-the-counter Brent crude prices toward $80/bbl. Given the magnitude of this geopolitical shock involving a major OPEC producer, it is overwhelmingly likely that spot prices will jump upon market open on Monday and remain well above the $71.32 baseline. 

For the short horizon of March 8 (effectively trading data from Friday, March 6), the shock will still be fresh and risk premiums will remain very high, making a prompt mean-reversion below $71.32 extremely unlikely. I estimate this at 0.92. 

For the March 31 horizon, there is more time for potential de-escalation, rapid diplomatic resolution, or further OPEC+ supply interventions (they announced a 206k bpd output increase starting in April). While prices usually retain a geopolitical risk premium for months after such a shock, the chance of a partial mean reversion or resolution increases with time. I estimate this at 0.82.

Thus, the estimate moves closer to 50%, set marginally positive at 52%.

The Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) sets the target range for the federal funds rate, which virtually determines the DFF. The Fed has been in an easing cycle, cutting rates steadily in 2025 until holding and stabilizing the DFF at 3.64% since late December 2025. At the January 28, 2026 meeting, the rate was held steady, but there were two dissents strongly advocating for an additional cut, signaling a dovish bias. The next FOMC meeting will take place on March 17-18, 2026. Because this meeting occurs after the first resolution date, no policy change can occur before March 8, meaning the rate will almost certainly remain completely flat at exactly 3.64% (absent an unprecedented random daily 1 bp spike). By the second resolution date (March 31), the FOMC will have met. Current market consensus confidently forecasts either a rate hold or another rate cut; a rate hike is extraordinarily unlikely given the ongoing economic trajectory. With a target range increase virtually impossible and month-end/quarter-end reporting fluctuations more likely to cause minor dips rather than spikes, any strict increase (>3.64%) on either date is highly improbable. The probability is set at the minimum allowable thresholds, slightly increasing for the latter date.

The outcome depends on whether the PMMS 30-year fixed rate mortgage average exceeds its 2026-02-26 value of 5.98%. For the March 8 resolution (corresponding to the March 5 PMMS reading), the rate is largely determined by 10-year Treasury yields from the prior week (late February/early March). By February 27, the 10-year Treasury yield had declined slightly to ~4.04%, continuing the downward momentum that drove the PMMS down to 5.98% in the first place. Therefore, a slight decrease or flat reading is more likely than a bounce for the March 5 release, making an increase less than 50% likely (estimated at 40%). For the March 31 resolution (which will capture readings up to March 26), the rate will reflect major upcoming data releases, including the March 6 Non-Farm Payrolls and March 11 CPI report, as well as the March 17-18 FOMC meeting. The greater uncertainty and the potential for mean reversion to the expert consensus forecast (which places 2026 rates in the "low 6% range") pushes the probability closer to 50%. With the current baseline of 5.98% sitting just below this psychological expectation, there is a reasonable possibility of a modest bounce towards 6% if inflation or labor data surprise to the upside. However, given the sustained downward trend of recent weeks, the probability remains slightly skewed against an increase.

The Chicago Fed's NFCI Credit Subindex is constructed using a dynamic factor model that aggregates numerous financial time series. By its nature, it is heavily smoothed and exhibits strong autocorrelation (momentum). Over the last 4 weeks leading up to the 2026-02-27 cutoff, the index has experienced persistent, accelerating increases: from a trough of -0.036 on Jan 30 to -0.011 on Feb 27. The first derivative reached an extremely high +0.009 in the latest week. For the March 8 resolution (a 1-week horizon), this momentum makes it highly probable the index will continue to climb and exceed -0.011. For the March 31 resolution (a 4-week horizon), uncertainty increases. However, examining the historical data from the past year shows that peaks and troughs in this index typically occur in cycles lasting 2 to 4 months (e.g., Aug-2025 to Dec-2025). Since the current upswing from the January trough has only lasted one month so far, the index will likely still be in its ascending phase or near its apex by late March. Thus, the March 27 reading corresponding to the March 31 date is also very likely to remain above the -0.011 threshold, although there is a larger margin of uncertainty in case a local cyclical reversal occurs earlier than expected.

The reference class is the short-term weekly fluctuations of the Federal Reserve's total assets. Historically prior to December 2025, the balance sheet was shrinking rapidly due to Quantitative Tightening (QT), giving a base rate probability of around 30-48% based on linear models and seasonality.

However, recent news confirms the Federal Reserve officially ended its balance sheet drawdown (QT) on December 1, 2025. Subsequently, the Fed announced a "technical buying" program to purchase $20 billion to $25 billion of Treasury bills per month to ensure ample market liquidity reserves. This structurally changes the time series from a negative drift to a positive drift of approximately +$5 to +$6 billion per week. 

Analyzing the actual FRED data (RESPPANWW) from January through February 2026 closely mirrors this structural shift: the total assets have been steadily increasing with an average weekly gain of roughly +$5.85 billion. The weekly volatility (std dev) is approximately $9.2 billion. 

Given this positive drift, the immediate first week of March (resolving 2026-03-08) has a statistically strong likelihood of continuing the upward trend and exceeding the late February baseline that acts as the Mar 1 value. Modeling this as a normal distribution parameterized by recent weekly changes gives a ~73% probability of being higher. 

In keeping with the prompt's strong explicit instruction that uncertainty must increase (meaning probabilities should be closer to 0.5) for more distant forecasts, I am adjusting the probability for the final 2026-03-31 date closer to the midpoint to reflect generic long-horizon uncertainties (such as interim policy changes or unexpected seasonal roll-offs), despite the cumulative positive drift from technical buying computationally favoring an even higher probability.

The weekly remittance series reflects the Fed's 'deferred asset' - its accumulated operating losses. When the Fed operates at a loss, this series grows more negative. The long-term trend has been negative, but the rate of decrease has slowed significantly in late 2025/early 2026, reflecting the Fed nearing profitability (e.g., due to lower interest expenses on reserves). 

Short-term (March 8): The most recent daily momentum in late February has been negative (-51, -262 in successive weeks). The first week of March lacks the major mid-quarter Treasury coupon payments (like those on Feb 15) that occasionally cause the series to spike upwards. Therefore, the immediate week-over-week change is likely to remain negative, warranting a lower probability (0.32) of an increase by March 8.

Medium-term (March 31): Over a four-week period, uncertainty increases. There is a higher chance that structural regime shifts (a transition to sustained Fed profitability) or end-of-quarter effects begin to overpower the weekly noise. As the Fed draws closer to positive net income, longer time horizons introduce more variance, pulling our estimate closer to 0.5. I estimate a 0.42 probability that the balance will be higher by the end of March compared to the March 1 baseline.

The value for the base date of March 1 will logically default to the newest preceding business day data, February 27, which was 0.0. Therefore, the question asks whether the value on the resolution dates (or their closest preceding business days) will be greater than 0.0. Over the past month (Feb 2026), 9 of 16 observations were non-zero, including occasional massive spikes corresponding to Standing Repo Facility (SRF) usage (e.g., $18.5B on Feb 17). This gives an empirical base rate of ~56% for any randomly chosen business day. By March 8 (evaluating the Friday, March 6 data), ongoing interbank liquidity tightness and routine small-value test operations support a moderately elevated probability (0.62) of an operation > 0.0.

For the more distant horizon of March 31, typically quarter-end dates have very high structural repo spikes. However, adhering to the system constraint that uncertainty must increase with the forecast horizon, the March 31 probability is drawn closer to 0.50 (0.55). Expanding outright Treasury purchases by late March would permanently inject reserves, materially curbing the banking sector's need to rely on the overnight SRF at the Q1 close.

Arjun Erigaisi's Wikipedia FIDE rating dropped significantly by February 28 to 2745 (down from 2775), reflecting a substantial rating loss at the Tata Steel Masters 2026 (completed early February, where he scored 4.5/13 against strong opposition, losing ~30 Elo). According to the text fetched from the Wikipedia page natively as of March 1, 2026, his exact listing is 2745. Consequently, the threshold to resolve YES requires him to have a listed Elo rating of >= 2772.45 on the given resolution dates. 

On March 8, the listing is virtually guaranteed to remain unchanged at 2745, as FIDE updates classical chess ratings on a strict monthly basis. On March 31, if the page has not updated to the April list, the rating persists at 2745; if it does update a day early to the April list, he would need to have gained approximately 28 Elo in a single month of classical tournament play. At the super-GM level, a +28 point turnaround in exactly one month requires phenomenal and extremely rare 2900+ performances over a high volume of games. Because the baseline of 2745 is accurate and monthly chess rating shifts of this magnitude are extremely uncommon, an increase meeting the 1% condition is highly improbable. Probabilities are set to the minimum constrained bound plus a slight increase for horizon.

Elite players virtually never play classical games in the immediate month prior to the Candidates, to focus on preparation. Consequently, she will have no classical games rated for the April list.
